excerpt_en: Today was a public holiday in Ireland, so even though it was Monday, school was off. So I spent the daytime working in my room. Lately, I've been getting offers for illustration design work, and one way or another
body_en:
Today was a public holiday in Ireland,

so even though it was Monday, school was off.

So I spent the daytime working in my room.

Lately, I've been getting offers for illustration design work,

and one way or another, I've been keeping busy.

On top of that, I was also preparing meeting materials for the Sagishima Project that we're working on back in Japan.

At the same time, I'm also editing the Masu Photo book for the American edition.

Once again, since my projects span so many different genres, I really need to manage everything well.

And I'm just barely pulling it off.

After dinner,

I went for a run for the first time in about a week.

This past week, I'd been coughing,

and whenever I ran, I'd start choking right away.

So to recover,

I hadn't been running at all.

But I've been taking the medicine I brought with me,

and I've been feeling a lot better,

so I went running through areas around my house that I hadn't explored yet.

About 10km.

The reason I've been running like this is

partly to build up stamina,

and also to train for the Medoc Marathon coming up next month.

To be honest, since it had been a while since I last ran,

my body felt a bit sluggish.

If I can keep a 7km pace the whole way,

even if I walk part of the way or drink some wine,

I should at least be able to finish.

My plan is to run about 20km, then cover the remaining 20km at a brisk walking pace while drinking wine, but who knows how that'll actually go?

At any rate, sometime this month,

I want to do a 20km run!

Today, while running through unfamiliar roads,

I found a route that's easy to run on,

so I'll use that when I do the 20km run.

title_en: Heading to a Social Event I Found on an App
excerpt_en: In the morning, I went to language school as usual. It's a 40-minute commute each way. At the language school in the Philippines, I was living in a dorm, so I could wake up, eat breakfast, take the elevator up, and be at class -- a wonderful system, but
body_en:
In the morning, I went to language school as usual.

It's a 40-minute commute each way.

At the language school in the Philippines,

I was living in a dorm,

so I could wake up, eat breakfast,

take the elevator up, and be at class --

a wonderful system,

but now I'm doing a homestay,

so that's not possible, which is tough lol

That said,

I do manage to get up properly,

and I attend classes seriously... sort of.

But as I mentioned before,

I really want to move up to a higher class level.

Since I'm planning to switch classes,

I haven't even bought the textbook that everyone else has lol

I'm the only one taking photos of my neighbor's textbook

and making do with that lol

I display the textbook on my iPad

and use the pen feature to work through the problems lol

From the outside, do I look like some poor guy who can't afford a textbook?

Or maybe people are just wondering why I won't buy one. lol

After class: meetings

Class ends at 1 PM,

and normally it's lunchtime.

But in my case,

that's when work starts.

FOMUS meetings

usually begin at 2 PM.

That's 10 PM in Japan time.

I'm nothing but grateful to the team members who make time for meetings at that hour.

Today's meeting was also productive.

After that, until the school closes at 5 PM,

I stay right where the meeting was held

and work on the parts I need to push forward.

Then at 5 PM, finally lunch!

Or rather, dinner at that point.

Everything in Ireland is expensive,

and there's no point in me splurging alone,

so today I went to McDonald's.

I tried a Twister.

Is it unusual to find Twisters at McDonald's?

By the way, this cost about 1,200 yen. lol

It's genuinely expensive,

but even so,

it's about 500 yen cheaper than other restaurants.

I worked here for about two and a half hours.

Heading to a meetup I found on an app

Now, here's the main part of today.

At the Japan-Ireland exchange event last week, someone told me about an app called "Meetup."

Events from all over the world are posted there,

and you can freely join them.

So,

I had downloaded it,

and I found out there was a meetup event specifically for making friends,

and since you can't learn anything without going,

I decided to give it a try.

The venue was a bar.

No entry fee,

just order a drink and mingle.

I arrived about 5 minutes after the start time,

and there were already quite a few people there.

I came alone,

and of course I didn't know anyone,

so I found a seat

and started talking to the people nearby.

It would be a little different if I had even one acquaintance there,

but that kind of comfort isn't allowed

on this Ireland expedition.

The goal is to get as many people as possible to know MaSU,

and from there, connect them to FOMUS.

When talking with people who speak Japanese, conversations flow relatively smoothly, but in English, since I'm not yet fluent, the back-and-forth is extremely difficult.

Every time I'm in a place like this, I'm painfully aware of how limited my English is.

That said,

this is an experience I could never have in Japan,

so of course, I plant myself there with confidence lol

Then,

a guy I happened to meet at last week's event

came up and talked to me.

He's someone who finds the FOMUS business really interesting, and we had all sorts of conversations.

Then he said, "Let's go join another group!" so I jumped at the chance and tagged along.

At a bar like this where Japanese won't get you anywhere,

suddenly joining a completely unfamiliar group

is actually quite difficult.

*Personally speaking

He has incredibly strong people skills and is the type who can get along with anyone,

so I figured I'd try to steal some of his techniques.

What I

learned here was:

just actively throw yourself in

and ask questions.

Then, once you find something in common,

build the conversation from there.

Before you know it, you're part of the group.

Something like that.

In Japan,

you tend to feel like

barging into a conversation is rude,

but this is Ireland.

The person who boldly joins in and drives the conversation

takes the lead in the group.

I wanted to develop this skill too, but fundamentally, this ability requires the "English proficiency" to understand what others are saying, respond to it, and keep the conversational ball rolling.

I realized that the problem stems precisely from lacking the most important capability needed to take the lead in a group.

Basically, nobody directs conversation at me.

The ones who get attention are usually the girls.

That's exactly why I need to develop the skill of initiating conversation myself,

getting responses, and smoothly connecting it back to my own topics.

That said, when it comes to conversation,

there isn't much business-related talk.

Because there aren't many business owners here.

It's mostly company employees

and people on vacation who gather at these events.

Still, if I can't hold my own in English with the people who gather here, then even if I attend gatherings of business owners, I'd just stand there like a statue unable to follow the conversation.

So I'll keep using places like this as practice grounds too.

In the end, today as well,

I managed to connect with people from various countries.

At bars like this, every encounter is a one-time thing,

and at the end you exchange Instagram accounts,

so SNS really is important.

Plus, we don't really take photos,

so remembering faces has to happen on the spot. lol

When I'm the only Japanese person there,

everyone's chatting away in fluent English,

and honestly, if it weren't for business,

I probably wouldn't bother coming to places like this.

Because there are plenty of things that are more fun and enjoyable.

But precisely because I'm here carrying FOMUS on my back,

running away from that situation is not an option for me,

and if I can't change this feeling of slight discomfort (which is just caused by my limited English),

there's no way I can spread FOMUS to the world in the first place.

In my case, I'm stronger in one-on-one situations

than in groups,

so I'll try to steer things in that direction too.

So,

it was a really productive event.

I'll keep actively participating

in meetup events like these,

working on improving my English

and expanding the FOMUS brand!

title_en: Getting People to Know Me Before the Content
excerpt_en: Well, it's been 10 days since I arrived in Ireland. The days are flying by. In the morning I attend language school, and after class, I work on FOMUS stuff on the rooftop, or if there's an interesting event organized by the school, I join
body_en:
Well,

it's been 10 days since I arrived in Ireland.

The days are flying by.

In the morning I attend language school,

and after class,

I work on FOMUS stuff on the rooftop,

or if there's an interesting event organized by the school, I join in.

In the evening,

if there's a meetup event, I attend it,

and if not, I go home and work on FOMUS activities.

Living like this,

the days really fly by in an instant.

Because of the high cost of living,

if I'm just attending school,

"I can skip lunch"

is the kind of thinking I've fallen into,

and lately,

I eat breakfast at home,

skip lunch,

and for dinner, either eat at home

or grab fast food

before heading to an event.

That's kind of how it's been going.

Is it a healthy diet?

Honestly, if you ask me, it's questionable,

but I still haven't found

any really good food in Ireland to begin with.

Right now I'm doing a homestay,

so I can't cook for myself,

but the simple tomato pasta I used to make

when I was cooking for myself in America and Spain

is honestly still the best thing I've had.

I really want to find cheap and tasty food here soon. lol

  • Another meetup this week

On Wednesdays, there's a Japan-Ireland exchange event

held every week in the city.

I went last week and it was fun,

so I went again this week.

Basically, if you don't

push yourself forward, you won't get into any conversations.

I wrote about this in yesterday's article,

so I'll spare the details,

but being proactive is key.

You might feel like

"it would be rude to interrupt,"

but that's just a momentary thing,

and nobody really cares lol

So, today I interacted with all sorts of people again.

Some of them were people I talked to last week!

By attending repeatedly like this,

you get more familiar faces,

and things gradually start to feel more comfortable.

Now, here's the main topic for today.

Over these 10 days,

I've attended numerous events,

but I haven't brought out the "masu" yet.

I always have a masu badge pinned to my chest,

but I haven't shown anyone a real masu.

I'm sure some people would say, "Just show them the product already!" and I totally get that,

but in my view, getting people to know "MaSU" the person

and making them think "This guy seems interesting!" is the most important thing,

and the product comes after that.

In other words, the person comes first.

Get people genuinely interested in "MaSU," then bring up work topics,

build a relationship,

and then introduce the content and services -- that's the flow.

I'm not sure if this approach is correct,

but that's how I'm operating right now.

For now, my mission is

to show my face at as many places as possible.

To get as many people as possible

to know about FOMUS,

I'll take it slow, be patient,

and aim for the top.

excerpt_en: Today, I'm going to write about the theme of "boosting productivity." For the first 3 months of my activities in Ireland, weekday mornings are spent at language school every day, and nothing much changes unless something special happens. Writing about the class content
body_en:
Today, I'm going to write about the theme of "boosting productivity."

For the first 3 months of my activities in Ireland,

weekday mornings are spent at language school every day,

and nothing much changes unless something special happens.

Writing about the class content here

wouldn't be interesting for me or for you readers, I think.

So,

let me talk about what happens after class.

Working on a certain document

I'm in Ireland,

but there are plenty of projects

currently in progress back in Japan too.

There's an opportunity to give a boost to one of them, and I'm working on the materials for that.

When it comes to creating documents,

you have to write text within certain guidelines,

compile the necessary paperwork,

and there's a lot to do.

Normally, creating these materials

would take a full day or even a week.

As I mentioned earlier,

for these 3 months, I can't work in the mornings (though I can respond to messages), so I've lost 4 hours of work time per day.

Of course, I also need to study to improve my own skills on top of that,

and there's FOMUS work too.

Creating these documents is obviously important,

but I want to minimize the time spent on it as much as possible.

So I've been using AI tools

to boost my productivity and efficiency.

Since I also serve as an AI utilization advisor, I need to keep studying the information that gets updated daily, and because I'm constantly playing around with these tools,

it goes without saying that I know how to use them well.

So,

once I understand

"this is what needs to be created,"

I can pull in the right AI tool for it

and give it the instructions to achieve my goal.

This gets about 70% of the document done.

Of course, there are subtle nuances and occasional mistakes, so in the end,

I go through everything and check it all myself,

but going from 0% to 70%

and going from 70% to 100%

are completely different levels of effort.

I'd say I'm pretty good

at using AI, if I do say so myself.

So,

this time too, I had AI handle more than half of the document creation.

It's like having the ultimate secretary.

This has dramatically boosted my productivity

and made my work much more efficient.

In this day and age,

the key is how you apply AI to your own activities.

Since such a powerful amplification tool has emerged,

there's no reason not to use it.

So,

by playing around with various AI tools,

I once again realized that I can do even more interesting activities than ever before!

title_en: Less Than a Month to Go
excerpt_en: A rare sunny day in Ireland. After class today, I did a bit of FOMUS work, then headed home. From there, I changed into my FOMUS uniform and went running! I've been running quite a lot this year, and the reason is
body_en:
A rare sunny day in Ireland.

After class today,

I did a bit of FOMUS work,

then headed home.

From there, I changed into my FOMUS uniform and

went running!

I've been running quite a lot this year,

and the reason is to train for the Medoc Marathon, which will be held on September 7th in Pauillac, France.

The features of the Medoc Marathon are:

(1) It's a full marathon

(2) Costumes are encouraged

(3) There's wine at the rest stops

Those are the key features.

I've never run a full marathon before,

and I never really wanted to,

but this year, various conditions aligned,

and I decided to enter.

I made this decision

back in February.

I was in Cebu at the time,

and that's where I paid the Medoc Marathon entry fee (for 3 people).

From that point on,

I developed a strong desire

to finish the Medoc Marathon,

that desire sprouted in me,

and I started practicing my running.

I've been doing regular training since then.

Today the weather was great -- a perfect "running day" --

so I went for a run.

The running spot is a peninsula north of my house.

"Howth"

Howth is also the last stop on the train line I always ride, and I'd been curious about what it was like, so I set it as my destination this time.

I never expected I'd be running there from home.

There were unexpectedly steep hills and mountain trails,

and some parts were pretty tough.

That said, since it was my first time there,

I was able to run with an exploratory mindset.

There were some mountain trails too, which surprised me,

but I managed to go all the way around the island.

This time it was about 18km.

Compared to the Medoc Marathon,

it's not even half the distance,

but it was good training.

All in all,

it was a great workout!

excerpt_en: 8/10 Saturday. Still tired from yesterday's training, I stayed in my room and worked. One of FOMUS's projects is the "Masu Photo book," and I worked on editing, selecting photos, and designing covers for the America and Spain photo books from before I came to Ireland
body_en:
8/10

Saturday.

Still tired from yesterday's training,

I stayed in my room and worked.

One of FOMUS's projects is

the "Masu Photo book,"

and before coming to Ireland, I had stayed in

America and Spain, so I worked on editing, selecting photos, and designing covers for those photo books.

Also, as a change of pace,

I used the latest AI

to turn illustrations into videos,

and even created BGM and put it all together.

I gave myself a strict time limit of just 30 minutes (because without a limit I'd keep going forever),

and I managed to produce something of decent quality.

Man, the latest AI tools are seriously fun.

What I'm showing here are illustrations,

but all of these become videos.

excerpt_en: 8/11 From midday, I attended a "Japan-Ireland meetup event" held every week in Dublin city center. This event has apparently been going on for quite a while. Actually, this event was the one I briefly mentioned in Ireland Activity Log #7
body_en:
8/11

From midday, I attended a

"Japan-Ireland meetup event"

held every week in Dublin city center.

This event has apparently been going on for quite a while.

Actually, this event was

the one I briefly mentioned in Ireland Activity Log #7.

On this day, I went right at

the start time,

and there were only about 4 people.

As I chatted with the people around me,

more and more people kept arriving,

and it turned into a big group.

There are a lot of Japanese people,

but there are also plenty of Irish people

who are interested in Japan,

and it's great not only for socializing

but also for improving my English.

This time too,

I was able to connect with lots of people.

Also, when you show up at this many events, you start running into the same people,

and that's pretty fun in its own way.

Although, there were cases where I remembered someone

but they didn't remember me,

which was a bit disappointing.

I'd been operating with the mindset of

getting people to know me without using the "masu,"

that's how I'd been operating lately,

so I guess I just end up being "one of many."

That's my mistake, honestly.

I think I should definitely start

showing off the "masu" after all. lol

Writing this now,

I realize first impressions are everything,

and it's better to leave a strong impact

right away so people remember you.

Since I have

a real, physical product to show,

I should have been using it aggressively.

The people I did show the masu to

all remembered me clearly,

so there's nothing more obvious than that lol

How much I can use English

outside of school

is a crucial part of improving,

so I'll keep actively

attending meetup events!

Played some random chopstick game

excerpt_en: My activities in Ireland have entered their 3rd week. Starting today, my language school class has leveled up, and I'm using more practical English. I'd been saying this since the first week, so I'm glad I was able to move up
body_en:
My activities in Ireland have entered their 3rd week.

Starting today,

my language school class has leveled up,

and I'm using more practical English.

I'd been saying this since the first week,

so I'm glad I was able to move up.

The reasons were that the classes were too easy,

and the other students' English was too low -- pairing up with them was personally tough.

But as of today, I'm free from that! lol

The textbook has changed too,

and it's now at just the right level for me.

I'll aim to move up to the next level

in about 2 more weeks,

and really focus on training my English.

What do I do after class?

Class ends at 1 PM,

so from there I worked on FOMUS business in the study room.

Normally it would be lunchtime,

but since all I'm doing is attending English class,

lunch isn't strictly necessary, if you ask me,

not really.

So I use lunchtime

as time to work on FOMUS instead.

On nights when there's a meetup event,

I can't make it home in time for dinner at my homestay,

so I need to eat in the city,

and after 5 PM, I head to McDonald's.

It costs about 800 to 1,000 yen each time.

As for today specifically,

from 4 PM I attended a social event at school.

I was surprised that the ratio of Japanese people was quite high,

but there were also Italians, Swiss people, and others,

and I got to chat with all sorts of people.

Since it's a school in Ireland,

people come from all over Europe,

ranging from young people to older folks.

The ones I get along with the most

are people about a decade older than me.

Stories from people with rich life experience are fascinating!

They also showed interest in FOMUS's activities,

and said they'd show me around

when I visit their countries!

#FriendsAreEverything

After that, I went home, ate dinner,

and went running for about an hour from 9 PM.

In Ireland, the sun sets really late.

I'm staying a bit outside Dublin city center, so in a way, there are tons of great running routes,

and it's an amazing environment for training lol

#TheClimateHereIsSoCoolUnlikeJapan

#IWearAnUltraLightDownDuringTheDay lol

Today I ran to the beach.

Running is training for the Medoc Marathon,

but it also makes me healthier as a bonus,

so it actually works out pretty well lol

Today I ran 10km.

When I got back,

I immediately took a shower,

then it was desk work from there.

So that's how I've been spending my days,

but from here on out, I'll also be

gradually starting Masu Photo shoots in Ireland!

Just like in the previous countries,

I'll use Instagram

to find people living in Ireland

and photograph them.

If things go well, I might connect with professional models

and be able to do something new,

so I'll approach this in a way that's only possible because I'm based here locally.

And with that,

Week 3 has begun!!!

title_en: Is It Easier When There Are Some Rules?
excerpt_en: So today, from the afternoon, I headed to a meetup event. Today was my first time at an event I found on the Meetup app. Of course, I went in solo. The venue was a bar. Here, "language exchange" was the theme
body_en:
So today, from the afternoon,

I headed to a meetup event.

Today was my first time at an event I found on the Meetup app.

Of course, I went in solo.

The venue was a bar.

Here, "language exchange" was the theme of the event, where you teach each other your languages --

cross-cultural exchange was the main focus.

Plus,

unlike the previous meetup events,

you pay an entry fee upfront and get assigned a seat.

The seats have numbers on them,

and you sit where you're told.

Since the organizer assigns the seats,

you don't need to search for someone to talk to after arriving.

You sit at your assigned seat and talk.

That's the rule.

Then after about 30 minutes, there's a seat shuffle.

It's a system that forces you to interact with new people.

I think this has both pros and cons -- the downside is that even if there's someone you want to talk to, you can't go over to them,

but for your first or second time,

you don't have to force yourself to jump into a conversation

because you're given a seat,

which makes it incredibly easy to participate.

In other words, just by having a few rules in place,

things unfold much more smoothly.

For Japanese people who struggle with approaching strangers, this might be a really great format.

I'm constantly checking how these systems work,

and thinking about what kind of event I would put together if I were the organizer.

My goal is to spread the masu brand "FOMUS," but for people overseas, it's hard to even get them interested in events centered around masu.

Well, that's the same in Japan too, honestly.

So,

by pairing it with an event,

I want to create a flow where people who come to the event also become interested in masu.

That's exactly why

I'm attending all sorts of events,

observing how Japanese people move in Ireland, and studying the behavior and personalities of people from other countries.

One thing that's certain is

that everyone loves beer.

Plus, attendees buy their own beer,

so there's no downside for the organizer.

No risk to bear either.

So if I know the headcount in advance,

I can plan accordingly,

and if I can generate a strong feeling of

"I want to attend this event!"

then I can pull off

hosting events in Ireland too.

I've been running events for 5 years now, after all,

so I can handle small to medium-sized events.

The only question is: how do I attract people?

That's the key.

There are various ways to go about it,

so I'll work out the details

and try hosting one in September.

Let's do this!!

title_en: Forcing Myself into a Business English Environment
excerpt_en: Today, in the morning I studied in my regular English class, and in the afternoon I worked on FOMUS as usual. Right now, I'm editing the FOMUS photo books for America and Spain. The American edition booklet has already been ordered for FOMUS. However, this time I switched the
body_en:
Today, in the morning I studied in my regular English class,

and in the afternoon I worked on FOMUS as usual.

Right now, I'm editing

the FOMUS photo books for America and Spain.

The American edition booklet

has already been ordered for FOMUS.

However, this time I switched from

the printing company I'd been using,

so I don't know the paper quality of the photo book yet.

Since it's a company that specializes in albums and such,

I don't think there'll be a problem,

but I can't send something to our sponsors

without confirming the quality first.

*Companies that became photo book sponsors through our crowdfunding campaign will receive booklets for free

So,

I'll check the quality first,

and if there are no issues, I'll place another order

and start shipping them out.

At the same time,

I'm also creating the web version of the photo book.

https://masuphoto.theshop.jp/

FOMUS's Masu Photo book comes in two formats:

a print edition and an online edition.

If I just put the print version online as-is,

some pages would feel awkward,

so I'm redesigning the layout for the web version too.

For this kind of work,

I'm going with my accumulated sense and intuition.

Diving into an environment where I'm forced to speak English

While I was working on the photo book,

I received a message.

It was an invitation from a Ukrainian man I met at the meetup event during my first week in Dublin.

He's a business owner who finds FOMUS (or rather, me) interesting. He wanted to talk in a quiet environment, and since opportunities like this are still rare overseas, I decided to go.

So,

from 6 PM, I talked business with him.

To expand FOMUS further,

more money is needed, and what should we do about that?

We talked about that for about 4 hours lol

All in English, of course.

What I was reminded of once again is

that my vocabulary is still far too limited.

I have things I want to say,

but I can't express them fluently in English.

And with his words too,

there are vocabulary gaps here and there,

so my comprehension ends up at about 70%.

If I could get that to 100%, we could have so much more interesting conversations!! That feeling is strong.

Still, spending about 4 hours

desperately trying to catch what he's saying and squeezing out my own thoughts in English -- you can't easily create that kind of environment, so this kind of opportunity was a huge personal learning experience.

This week, every day

I have meetup events lined up,

and I want to absorb as many ideas and hints as possible for hosting events overseas and expanding my community!
